does: This paper proposes HPRO, a hierarchical progressive reward optimization framework for improving emotional expressiveness in LLM-based text-to-speech systems. The core contribution is addressing two structural problems in preference-driven TTS optimization: information conflict (where content and emotion compete in shared latent spaces, causing reward hacking) and scale gap (sparse sentence-level rewards struggling to guide dense frame-level generation). The authors introduce the HD-Emo codec, a differentiable reward model that separates speech into distinct content and style preference tokens, isolating emotional optimization from semantic constraints. HPRO then progressively aligns frame-level generations with sentence-level emotional preferences through this structured preference space, enabling more emotionally expressive outputs while maintaining semantic fidelity.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in LLM-based TTS models that achieve high naturalness through scaling and architectural improvements, but identifies a fundamental limitation: standard supervised fine-tuning converges to averaged prosody lacking emotional nuance. It extends preference-based optimization approaches that have proven effective in other domains (like RLHF for language models) to the speech synthesis setting. The paper responds to prior work attempting to inject emotion into TTS systems while recognizing that naive preference optimization fails due to competing optimization signals. It connects to broader research on disentangled representations in speech processing and hierarchical reward modeling, drawing parallels to multi-level supervision strategies that have appeared in video generation and long-sequence modeling tasks.
enables: This framework enables more emotionally expressive speech synthesis that maintains semantic accuracy, opening pathways for expressive voice conversion, personalized emotional TTS, and more natural human-computer interaction. The HD-Emo codec's structured preference extraction could generalize to other speech tasks requiring disentangled control of content and paralinguistic features. The hierarchical progressive alignment mechanism points toward addressing similar scale gaps in other sequence generation problems where local decisions must respect global constraints. Future work could extend this to multi-speaker emotional synthesis, real-time adaptation to contextual emotion, and integration with dialogue systems where emotional expressiveness enhances narrative coherence and user engagement.
